question: REST basics — methods, status codes, idempotency.
answer:
GET read, POST create, PUT replace, PATCH partial update, DELETE remove. GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent; POST is not.

Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 bad request, 401 unauthenticated, 403 forbidden, 404 not found, 500 server error.
tip: 401 = 'who are you?'; 403 = 'I know you, but no'.
deep:
The methods, and the two properties that matter

MethodSafe?Idempotent?Use
GET✔✔read
POST✘✘create
PUT✘✔replace whole resource
PATCH✘✘*partial update
DELETE✘✔remove
  • Safe — doesn't modify anything. GET must never have side effects (a GET /deleteUser?id=1 is a real bug — crawlers and prefetchers will fire it).
  • Idempotent — same request N times = same end state as once. Not "same response".

Idempotency is the question they're really asking

PUT /users/1  { name: 'Ann' }   x3  -> name is 'Ann'.  Same state. IDEMPOTENT.
POST /users   { name: 'Ann' }   x3  -> THREE users.    NOT idempotent.
DELETE /users/1                 x3  -> gone (then 404). Same STATE. IDEMPOTENT.

DELETE catches people: the second call returns 404, a different response — but the resulting state is identical, and that's the definition.

Why it matters practically: idempotent requests are safe to retry. That's exactly why you can blanket-retry a GET or PUT on a network blip, but retrying a POST may create duplicate orders. It's the same reasoning behind choosing concatMap over switchMap for writes. If a POST must be retry-safe, use an Idempotency-Key header so the server dedupes.

*PATCH is idempotent if it sets absolute values ({ status: 'done' }) but not if it's relative ({ increment: 1 }).

Status codes — the ones you must know cold

CodeMeaningNote
200 / 201 / 204OK / Created / No Content201 returns a Location header
400Bad Requestyour payload is malformed
401Unauthorized= unauthenticated. Who are you?
403Forbiddenwe know you; you're not allowed
404 / 409 / 422Not Found / Conflict / Unprocessable409 = version conflict
429Too Many Requestsrate limited — back off
500 / 503Server Error / Unavailableretry these, not 4xx

401 vs 403 is the classic trap: 401 means "authenticate" (refresh the token, redirect to login); 403 means "you're authenticated and still can't" (don't retry, don't redirect — show a message). Wiring an interceptor to log out on 403 is a real bug.

What makes an API RESTful

  • Resources, not verbs — POST /users, not POST /createUser. Nouns in the URL; the verb is the method.
  • Stateless — every request carries its own auth; the server keeps no session. That's what lets you scale horizontally behind a load balancer.
  • Plural collections — /users/1/orders.
  • Filtering/paging in the query string, not the path: /users?page=2&sort=name.

How to say it in an interview

“Resources as nouns, HTTP methods as the verbs, and stateless requests. The concept worth being precise about is idempotency — same request N times leaves the same state, which is why GET, PUT and DELETE are safe to retry and POST isn't, and why a retried POST can create duplicate orders unless you send an Idempotency-Key. On status codes the one people get wrong is 401 versus 403: 401 is unauthenticated so you refresh or redirect, 403 is authenticated but not permitted, so retrying or logging out is wrong.”

question: Explain the JWT authentication flow.
answer: User logs in → server returns a signed JWT (header.payload.signature). The client stores it and sends it as Authorization: Bearer <token>. The server verifies the signature and expiry on each request — no server-side session needed (stateless).
tip: The payload is only base64-encoded, not encrypted — never put secrets in it.
deep:
The structure

header.payload.signature      // three base64url segments joined by dots

eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9  .  eyJzdWIiOiIxIiwicm9sZSI6ImFkbWluIn0  .  4pcPyMD0...
{alg, typ}                 {sub, role, exp, iat}                 HMAC/RSA

The payload is base64, NOT encrypted. Anyone can paste the token into jwt.io and read every claim. Base64 is encoding, not encryption — so never put anything secret in a JWT: no PII, no passwords, no internal ids you don't want leaked.

What the signature gives you is integrity, not confidentiality: change one character of the payload and the signature no longer matches, so the server rejects it. You can read it; you can't forge it without the key.

The flow

1. POST /login {user, pass}  ->  server verifies, signs a JWT, returns it
2. Client stores it
3. Every request:  Authorization: Bearer <token>
4. Server verifies the SIGNATURE + exp — no DB lookup needed  ← the whole point
5. On 401 -> use the refresh token to get a new access token

Why JWTs exist: statelessness

A session cookie requires the server to look up a session store on every request. A JWT is self-contained — the server verifies the signature with a key it already holds and trusts the claims. No shared session store, so any instance behind a load balancer can serve any request. That's the entire selling point.

And the cost of statelessness: you can't revoke it

A signed JWT is valid until exp, full stop. Ban a user, and their token keeps working. Log out, and the token still works if someone copied it. There's no server-side "delete the session" — because there is no session.

Mitigations (this is the senior part of the answer):

  • Short-lived access tokens (5–15 min) + a long-lived refresh token. Damage window is minutes.
  • A denylist of revoked token ids — but now you're hitting a store on every request, which gives back the statelessness you bought.
  • A tokenVersion claim bumped on logout/password-change.

Storage — the question behind the question

localStoragehttpOnly cookie
Readable by JSyes → XSS steals itno
Sent automaticallyno (you add the header)yes → CSRF risk
Mitigation—SameSite=Strict + CSRF token

The common answer "store it in localStorage" is the wrong one: any XSS — including from a compromised npm dependency — reads it instantly. The better answer is an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie for the refresh token, keeping the access token in memory only.

The alg: none attack

Historically, libraries would accept a token whose header said {"alg":"none"} and skip verification entirely — trivial forgery. Always pin the expected algorithm server-side. It's a great example of why you never hand-roll JWT verification.

In Angular

// interceptor attaches the token; refreshes once on 401
intercept(req, next) {
  const authReq = req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: `Bearer ${this.token}` }});
  return next.handle(authReq).pipe(
    catchError(e => e.status === 401 ? this.refreshAndRetry(req, next) : throwError(() => e))
  );
}

How to say it in an interview

“Header, payload, signature — base64, so the payload is readable by anyone; the signature only guarantees integrity. That means no secrets in the claims. The value is statelessness: the server verifies a signature instead of hitting a session store, so any instance can serve any request. The trade-off is you can't revoke one — it's valid until it expires — which is why you use short-lived access tokens with a refresh token. And I wouldn't put it in localStorage; any XSS reads it. An httpOnly SameSite cookie for the refresh token, access token in memory.”

question: What is CORS and how do you fix it?
answer: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser rule that blocks requests to a different origin unless the server opts in with Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Fix it on the server; in dev you can use a proxy.
tip: CORS is enforced by the browser, so the fix always lives on the server.
deep:
What it actually is

CORS is a browser mechanism that relaxes the Same-Origin Policy. Two framings people get backwards:

  • It's not a server-side restriction — the request usually reaches your server and the server responds normally. The browser then inspects the response headers and, if they don't permit your origin, refuses to hand the response to JavaScript. The Network tab shows a 200; your code gets an error. That's why "it works in Postman" — Postman isn't a browser and doesn't enforce it.
  • It doesn't protect your server. curl, Postman, and any backend ignore it entirely. It protects users — stopping evil.com's JS from reading your bank's authenticated responses.

What counts as an origin

scheme :// host : port      — all three must match

https://app.com        vs  http://app.com          ✘ scheme
https://app.com        vs  https://api.app.com     ✘ host (subdomain!)
https://app.com        vs  https://app.com:8080    ✘ port

Simple vs preflighted

// SIMPLE — sent straight away
GET/HEAD/POST + only "safe" headers + content-type of
form-urlencoded | multipart/form-data | text/plain

// PREFLIGHTED — browser sends OPTIONS FIRST, asking permission
OPTIONS /api/users
  Origin: https://app.com
  Access-Control-Request-Method: PUT
  Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization, content-type

// server must answer:
  Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.com
  Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, PUT, POST
  Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization, content-type
  Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400        ← cache the preflight; skips it next time

Two things that surprise people: sending Content-Type: application/json or an Authorization header triggers a preflight — so almost every real API call is preflighted. And a preflight doubles your round trips until Access-Control-Max-Age caches it.

The credentials rule

// Client
fetch(url, { credentials: 'include' })        // or Angular: withCredentials: true

// Server MUST then:
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.com  // an EXACT origin — '*' is REJECTED
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true

Allow-Origin: * is illegal with credentials. The browser refuses it outright — otherwise any site could make authenticated requests on your behalf and read the response. You must echo the specific origin. This is the #1 CORS gotcha in production.

How you actually fix it

  • Fix the server — it's a server-header problem. Only the server can grant access.
  • Dev proxy — Angular's proxy.config.json makes the browser see one origin, so CORS never applies. This is why it works in dev and breaks in prod.
  • Same origin in prod — serve the API under /api on the app's domain via a reverse proxy. No CORS at all.
  • Not a browser extension or --disable-web-security. That "fixes" your machine and nobody else's.
// proxy.config.json — dev only
{ "/api": { "target": "http://localhost:3000", "secure": false } }

How to say it in an interview

“It's a browser relaxation of the same-origin policy. The key insight is the request usually reaches the server and the server replies fine — the browser just refuses to give the response to JS unless the headers allow the origin, which is why it works in Postman. So it protects users, not the server. Anything with a JSON content-type or an auth header gets preflighted with an OPTIONS, so I cache that with Access-Control-Max-Age. The gotcha that bites in prod is that Allow-Origin: * is rejected when credentials are included — you have to echo the exact origin. In dev I use the CLI proxy; in prod I'd rather serve the API same-origin behind a reverse proxy.”

question: HTTP vs HTTPS, and what is TLS?
answer: HTTPS is HTTP over TLS, which encrypts traffic, verifies the server's identity via certificates, and prevents tampering. It's required for PWAs, service workers, and modern browser features.
tip: HTTPS gives you confidentiality, integrity, and authentication of the server.

question: GET vs POST — key differences.
answer: GET — params in the URL, cacheable, idempotent, no body; for reads. POST — data in the body, not cached, not idempotent; for creating/side-effecting operations. Never send sensitive data in a GET query string.
tip: If refreshing the page shouldn't repeat the action, it shouldn't be a GET.

question: Authentication vs authorization.
answer: Authentication — proving who you are (login). Authorization — what you're allowed to do (roles/permissions). AuthN comes first, then AuthZ.
deep:
The distinction

  • Authentication (AuthN) — who are you? Proving identity: password, OTP, biometric, SSO.
  • Authorization (AuthZ) — what may you do? Checking permission, after identity is established.

Authentication always comes first: you can't decide what someone's allowed to do before you know who they are. Airport analogy: passport = authentication (you are who you claim), boarding pass = authorization (you may board this flight, in this seat).

Where it shows up in HTTP

401 Unauthorized403 Forbidden
Really meansunauthenticated (misnamed!)authenticated, not permitted
Causeno/expired tokenwrong role
Client shouldrefresh token → loginshow a message
Retry?after refreshnever — it'll fail identically

The HTTP spec's naming is genuinely wrong: 401 is about authentication despite being called "Unauthorized". Interviewers love this. And it's practical — an interceptor that logs users out on a 403 will boot people for clicking one admin-only button.

Authorization models

  • RBAC (role-based) — permissions attach to roles: admin, editor, viewer. Simple, most common.
  • ABAC (attribute-based) — rules over attributes: "editors may edit docs in their own department during business hours". Flexible, harder to reason about.
  • ACL — per-resource lists, like file sharing.
  • Ownership — "you may edit your own post". Extremely common and easy to forget.

The security point that matters most

// Angular guard — UX ONLY. Not security.
canActivate() { return this.auth.isAdmin(); }

Client-side authorization is not authorization. A route guard just stops the menu item rendering. Anyone can open DevTools, edit the JS, or simply curl your API directly — the browser is fully under the attacker's control. The server must re-check every rule on every request.

The classic vulnerability is IDOR — Insecure Direct Object Reference:

GET /api/orders/1001   -> my order   ✔
GET /api/orders/1002   -> someone else's order... does the server check ownership?

Hiding the link in the UI protects nobody. Saying "guards are UX, the API enforces it" is the answer that marks you as someone who's thought about security.

Angular in practice

// authN — is there a valid session?
export const authGuard = () => inject(AuthService).isLoggedIn() || inject(Router).parseUrl('/login');

// authZ — does the role permit this route?
export const adminGuard = () => inject(AuthService).hasRole('admin') || inject(Router).parseUrl('/403');

How to say it in an interview

“Authentication is who you are, authorization is what you're allowed to do — passport versus boarding pass, and authN always comes first. In HTTP it's 401 versus 403, and the naming is misleading: 401 actually means unauthenticated, so you refresh or redirect, while 403 means authenticated but not permitted, so retrying is pointless. The important part is that client-side guards are UX only — anyone can bypass the browser and call the API directly, so the server has to enforce every rule. Otherwise you get IDOR, where changing an id in the URL returns someone else's data.”

question: How does browser/HTTP caching work?
answer: Servers send caching headers: Cache-Control (max-age, no-cache), ETag/Last-Modified for revalidation. The browser reuses cached responses until they expire, then revalidates. CDNs cache at the edge for speed.
tip: Fingerprint filenames (app.abc123.js) so you can cache forever and bust on change.
deep:
The two questions caching answers

  1. Can I skip the request entirely? → freshness (Cache-Control: max-age)
  2. Can I skip the download? → validation (ETag → 304)

The first costs zero network. The second still round-trips but returns an empty body. Understanding that these are different is the core of the answer.

Cache-Control — the directives that matter

DirectiveMeans
max-age=3600fresh for 1h — no request at all
no-cachecache it, but revalidate every time ← misleading name!
no-storenever write it to disk — the real "don't cache"
privatebrowser only, not CDNs/proxies
publicCDNs may cache it
immutablenever revalidate — for hashed filenames
s-maxagemax-age, but for shared caches only

no-cache does not mean "don't cache" — it means "always revalidate before using". The one that means don't store is no-store. This is the most-asked CORS-style trick question in the caching space, and it matters: putting no-cache on a page with personal data still writes it to disk.

Validation: ETag / Last-Modified

// First response
200 OK
ETag: "abc123"

// Next request — browser asks "still abc123?"
GET /api/users
If-None-Match: "abc123"

// Server:
304 Not Modified          ← empty body! Headers only. Browser reuses its copy.

A 304 still costs a round trip but saves the payload — worth it for large or slow-changing resources. ETag is a content hash (exact); Last-Modified is a timestamp (1-second granularity, so weaker).

The pattern that makes SPAs fast

index.html          Cache-Control: no-cache        ← always revalidate (tiny)
main.a1b2c3.js      Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable
styles.d4e5f6.css   Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable

This is cache busting via content hashing, and it's why Angular's build fingerprints filenames. The HTML is always checked (it's ~2KB), and it points at hashed assets that can be cached forever — because if the content changes, the filename changes, so there's nothing to invalidate. You get permanent caching and instant deploys simultaneously.

Get this backwards — cache index.html for a year — and users are stuck on the old app referencing deleted bundle filenames. That's a real outage.

The layers

Browser memory → browser disk → Service Worker → CDN → server. A Service Worker is the one you program (Angular's @angular/pwa), and it's the only one that works offline.

Cache invalidation

The old joke — "there are only two hard things: cache invalidation and naming things" — is really about this: once you've told a cache something is fresh for a year, you cannot take it back. There's no "please forget that". Which is exactly why content-hashed filenames win: you never invalidate, you just change the URL.

How to say it in an interview

“Two mechanisms: freshness with max-age, which skips the request entirely, and validation with an ETag, where you still round-trip but get a 304 with no body. The naming trap is no-cache — it means revalidate every time, not don't store; that's no-store. The pattern I use for an SPA is no-cache on index.html and immutable year-long caching on content-hashed bundles — the filename changes when the content does, so you never need to invalidate, which is the hard part.”

question: SPA vs SSR vs SSG.
answer:
• SPA — renders in the browser; fast navigation, weaker initial SEO/first paint.
  • SSR — server renders HTML per request; better SEO and first paint (Angular Universal).
  • SSG — pre-render at build time; fastest, best for static content.
tip: SSR/SSG mainly help SEO and time-to-first-paint for content sites.
deep:
Where the HTML is built

SPA / CSRSSRSSG
Renderedin the browseron the server, per requestat build time
First paintslow (blank → JS → fetch)fastfastest (CDN)
SEOweakestgoodgood
Server costnone (static files)per requestnone
Data freshnesslivelivestale until rebuild
Best fordashboards, internal appse-commerce, personalisedblogs, docs, marketing

The SPA problem SSR solves

// What a crawler / a slow phone first receives from an SPA:
<body><app-root></app-root><script src="main.js"></script></body>
// Empty. Then: download JS -> parse -> bootstrap -> fetch data -> render.

Nothing is visible until the JS lands and runs — bad LCP on a slow network, and weak SEO because crawlers may not wait for JS. SSR sends real HTML on the first response, so content paints immediately.

Hydration — where SSR's complexity lives

SSR sends HTML, then the JS loads and Angular takes over the existing DOM. Historically it destroyed and re-rendered everything, causing a visible flicker. Angular 16+ has non-destructive hydration (provideClientHydration()), which reuses the server DOM instead.

The gap between "HTML visible" and "JS ready" is the uncanny valley: the page looks done but clicks do nothing. So SSR improves perceived load and FCP/LCP, but can make TTI worse — you shipped HTML and the same JS bundle. That nuance is the senior answer.

The SSR gotchas that break builds

// ✘ There is no `window` on the server -> ReferenceError
const w = window.innerWidth;

// ✔ guard it
if (isPlatformBrowser(this.platformId)) { const w = window.innerWidth; }

window, document, and localStorage don't exist in Node. Any library touching them at import time breaks SSR. This is the most common real-world SSR pain.

The modern answer: it's per-route, not per-app

The framing "pick one" is outdated. Angular 17+ ships hybrid rendering:

export const serverRoutes: ServerRoute[] = [
  { path: '',          renderMode: RenderMode.Prerender },  // marketing -> SSG
  { path: 'product/:id', renderMode: RenderMode.Server },   // SEO + live -> SSR
  { path: 'dashboard', renderMode: RenderMode.Client }      // private -> CSR
];

Prerender the landing page, SSR the product pages, CSR the logged-in dashboard — where SEO is irrelevant and SSR would just cost you servers. Add ISR (regenerate a static page every N seconds) and the SSG "stale data" weakness mostly disappears.

How to choose

  • Public + SEO + content rarely changes → SSG. Nothing beats a CDN file.
  • Public + SEO + personalised or live → SSR.
  • Behind a login → CSR. No crawler, no benefit, no server bill.

How to say it in an interview

“It's about where the HTML is produced — browser, server per request, or build time. An SPA ships an empty shell, so first paint waits on JS, which hurts LCP and SEO; SSR sends real HTML then hydrates. The nuance is that SSR improves perceived performance and FCP but can worsen TTI, since you ship HTML and the same bundle — and there's a window where it looks interactive but isn't. Practically I don't pick one app-wide any more: Angular 17 does hybrid rendering, so I'd prerender marketing pages, SSR the SEO-critical dynamic ones, and leave the authenticated dashboard client-rendered.”

question: What is Big-O and name common complexities.
answer: Big-O describes how runtime/space grows with input size (worst case). Common: O(1) constant, O(log n) binary search, O(n) linear scan, O(n log n) good sorts, O(n²) nested loops.
tip: A HashMap lookup is O(1); replacing a nested loop with a map often turns O(n²) into O(n).
deep:
What it is

Big-O describes how runtime grows with input size, ignoring constants and hardware. It's about the shape of the curve, not the speed — which is why an O(n²) can beat an O(n log n) on small inputs, and why it stops mattering at scale.

ONamen=1,000 →Example
O(1)constant1hash lookup, array index
O(log n)logarithmic~10binary search, balanced tree
O(n)linear1,000a loop, find
O(n log n)linearithmic~10,000sorting
O(n²)quadratic1,000,000nested loop
O(2ⁿ)exponential💀naive recursive fib

The jump from n log n to n² at n=1,000 is 100×. That's why "it worked in dev with 20 rows" turns into a production incident with 20,000.

The hidden O(n) that catches people

// Looks O(n). Is O(n²).
for (const user of users) {              // n
  const order = orders.find(o => o.userId === user.id);   // ← n INSIDE the loop
}

// O(n) — build a lookup first
const byUser = new Map(orders.map(o => [o.userId, o]));   // n
for (const user of users) {                                // n
  const order = byUser.get(user.id);                       // O(1)
}

A nested find/includes/indexOf is the most common real-world O(n²) — it doesn't look like a nested loop, because the inner loop is hidden in a method call. Trading memory for a Map is the standard fix, and it's exactly why NgRx's `@ngrx/entity` normalises to a dictionary.

The rules

  • Drop constants — O(2n) → O(n). Two sequential loops are still O(n).
  • Drop lower terms — O(n² + n) → O(n²).
  • Nested loops multiply; sequential loops add.
  • Different inputs get different letters: two separate arrays is O(n + m), not O(n).

Space complexity counts too

The Map fix above is O(n) space to buy O(n) time. That's the classic trade — and memoisation, caching, and lookup tables are all the same bargain. Mentioning space unprompted is a good signal.

Best / average / worst

Big-O is technically the upper bound. Quicksort is O(n log n) average but O(n²) worst case; a hash map is O(1) average but O(n) worst (all keys colliding) — which is exactly why Java 8 treeifies long HashMap chains, capping it at O(log n).

Where it actually matters in frontend

Honest framing: you rarely implement algorithms — you choose data structures. Using an array where a Map belongs, calling find inside a loop over 10k rows, or re-sorting on every change-detection cycle. Those are the frontend Big-O bugs.

How to say it in an interview

“It's how runtime scales with input, dropping constants — the shape of the curve, not the speed. The one I actually hit in frontend code is the accidental O(n²): a find inside a loop looks linear because the inner loop is hidden in the method call. Building a Map first makes it O(n), trading O(n) space for it. At a thousand items that's a hundred-fold difference, which is why something fine with twenty rows in dev falls over in production.”

question: Array vs Linked List vs Hash Table — tradeoffs.
answer:
• Array — O(1) index access, O(n) insert/delete in the middle.
  • Linked list — O(1) insert/delete at a known node, O(n) access.
  • Hash table — O(1) average lookup/insert, unordered.
tip: Pick the structure by the operation you do most: lookups → hash, ordered access → array.
deep:
The trade-off table

OperationArrayLinked ListHash Table
Access by indexO(1)O(n)—
Lookup by key/valueO(n)O(n)O(1) avg
Insert/delete at frontO(n)O(1)—
Insert/delete at endO(1)*O(1)—
Insert/delete in middleO(n)O(1)†—
Memorycompact+ pointer per node+ empty buckets
Ordered?✔✔✘

*amortised — see below. †O(1) only if you already hold the node; finding it is O(n).

Why each is what it is

  • Array — one contiguous block, so element i is at base + i × size: pure arithmetic, O(1). But inserting at the front means shifting every element — O(n).
  • Linked list — nodes scattered on the heap, each pointing to the next. No shifting, but no arithmetic either: reaching index 500 means walking 500 pointers.
  • Hash table — hash the key to a bucket index. Gives up ordering and memory to buy O(1) lookup.

The dirty secret: linked lists lose in practice

Their O(1) middle-insert is real but usually unreachable — you pay O(n) to find the position first. Worse, arrays win on cache locality: contiguous memory means the CPU prefetches the next elements, while a linked list's pointer-chasing causes a cache miss per node. A cache miss is ~100× a cache hit, so an "O(n) array shift" routinely beats an "O(1) linked-list insert" on real hardware for anything but huge n.

Saying this is a strong signal — it shows you know Big-O ignores constants, and constants sometimes decide the winner.

The JavaScript reality

[1, 2, 3]           // NOT a real array — a JS "array" is an object with
                    // integer-ish keys. V8 optimises it into a real
                    // contiguous array only while it stays "packed" —
                    // same types, no holes.

arr[10000] = 1;     // creates a sparse/"holey" array -> V8 DEOPTIMISES it
delete arr[0];      // same — makes it holey. Use splice().

So push is amortised O(1) (the backing store doubles and copies when full), but you can silently lose the fast path. And JS has no built-in linked list — if you want one you build it, which is itself a hint you probably don't need one.

What to actually reach for

  • Array — the default. Ordered, indexable, cache-friendly.
  • Map — lookup by key. Real O(1), any key type, keeps insertion order, and unlike an object it has no prototype keys to collide with.
  • Set — uniqueness / membership. set.has(x) is O(1) versus arr.includes(x) at O(n) — the single easiest real-world win.
  • Linked list — essentially never in app code. It's inside LRU caches and schedulers.

How to say it in an interview

“Arrays are contiguous, so index access is arithmetic — O(1) — but inserting at the front shifts everything. Linked lists flip that: O(1) insert if you hold the node, but O(n) to reach it. Hash tables trade ordering and memory for O(1) lookup. In practice arrays usually beat linked lists even where Big-O says otherwise, because contiguous memory is cache-friendly and pointer-chasing isn't. In JS I'd just use an array by default and a Map or Set the moment I'm doing lookups — swapping includes in a loop for a Set is the easiest real performance win there is.”

question: What are the SOLID principles?
answer:
• S — Single Responsibility (one reason to change).
  • O — Open/Closed (open to extension, closed to modification).
  • L — Liskov Substitution (subtypes replace base types safely).
  • I — Interface Segregation (small, focused interfaces).
  • D — Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions).
tip: Angular's DI is a direct application of the Dependency Inversion principle.
deep:
SOLID in an Angular/TypeScript codebase

Interviewers ask this of frontend devs to see whether you can apply it to components and services rather than recite Java examples. Anchor every principle to Angular.

S — Single Responsibility

// ✘ A component doing four jobs
export class UserListComponent {
  users: User[] = [];
  async ngOnInit() {
    const res = await fetch('/api/users');        // 1. HTTP
    this.users = (await res.json())
      .filter(u => u.active)                      // 2. business rules
      .sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name)); // 3. sorting
    localStorage.setItem('users', ...);           // 4. persistence
  }
}

Four reasons to change; untestable without mocking the world. Split: component renders, UserService fetches, a selector/pipe derives.

The frontend smell: a component over ~200 lines, or one whose name contains "And".

O — Open/Closed

// ✘ every new type edits this switch
getIcon(type: string) {
  switch (type) { case 'pdf': return 'file-pdf'; case 'doc': return 'file-word'; }
}

// ✔ open for extension — add a config entry, change no code
const ICONS: Record<FileType, string> = { pdf: 'file-pdf', doc: 'file-word' };

In Angular this is content projection (ng-content) and DI tokens: extend behaviour by providing something new, not by editing the component.

L — Liskov Substitution

A subtype must work anywhere its parent does. The frontend version usually isn't classes — it's components honouring a contract: if BaseInputComponent emits valueChange on every keystroke, a subclass that only emits on blur breaks every parent silently. Red flag: overriding a method to throw, or to do nothing.

I — Interface Segregation

// ✘ a fat input surface — most consumers pass 3 of 12
@Input() config!: { showHeader; showFooter; showToolbar; enableSort; ... };

// ✔ focused inputs with sane defaults, or split the component

Also: don't force a consumer to depend on methods it never calls. A 12-@Input component is usually several components.

D — Dependency Inversion ← the one Angular is built on

// ✘ depends on a concretion — impossible to test without a real network
export class OrderService {
  private http = new HttpClient(...);
}

// ✔ depends on an abstraction, injected
export class OrderService {
  private repo = inject(ORDER_REPOSITORY);   // an InjectionToken
}

// test:
providers: [{ provide: ORDER_REPOSITORY, useValue: mockRepo }]

Angular's DI is dependency inversion. That's the whole reason you can swap a real service for a spy in TestBed. If asked "why does Angular have DI?", this is the answer — and it links straight to testability.

The honest caveat

SOLID came from 1990s OO; it's guidance, not law. Applied dogmatically you get an interface per class and indirection nobody can follow. The two that pay off daily in frontend are SRP and DIP; the rest are lenses you reach for when something feels wrong.

How to say it in an interview

“In Angular terms: single responsibility means the component renders and the service fetches — a component doing HTTP, filtering and persistence has four reasons to change and can't be tested. Dependency inversion is the one Angular is literally built on — DI means I depend on an injection token rather than new-ing a dependency, which is exactly what lets TestBed swap in a mock. Open/closed shows up as content projection and config-driven behaviour instead of a switch you edit for every new type. I treat them as lenses rather than rules — SRP and DIP earn their keep daily, the others when something already smells.”

question: Explain Agile / Scrum basics.
answer: Scrum works in fixed sprints (1–4 weeks). Ceremonies: sprint planning, daily stand-up, review (demo), and retrospective. Roles: Product Owner (backlog), Scrum Master (process), and the development team.
tip: Stand-ups answer three things: what I did, what I'll do, what's blocking me.
